Title: Solution of the multistep pathway for assembly of corynanthean, strychnos, iboga, and aspidosperma monoterpenoid indole alkaloids from 19E-geissoschizine
Journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: precursor monomer for anticancer dimeric alkaloids such as anhydrovinblastine/vincristine
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: VIGS; in vitro pathway reconstitution with recombinant protein; metabolite profiling by LC-MS; heterologous expression in E. coli; assays with C. roseus and Tabernaemontana litoralis leaf proteins
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: major hydrolase-family transcript in C. roseus leaves (about 58% of HL transcripts)
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: promotes catharanthine formation from O-acetylstemmadenine-derived reactive intermediate; silencing strongly reduces catharanthine and increases vindoline relative abundance
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. VIGS targeting HL1 reduced catharanthine by about 80.1%. 2. Recombinant HL1 converted O-acetylstemmadenine-derived intermediate toward catharanthine in NADPH-dependent leaf protein assays. 3. In T. litoralis extracts, HL1 redirected flux from tabersonine toward catharanthine.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: VIGS in leaves; NADPH-dependent in vitro assays
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: HL1 likely acts after an uncharacterized NADPH-dependent step on an O-acetylated secodine-type intermediate; it is not a canonical pathway enzyme class or TF, so categorized here as Common_Gene.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Silencing altered branch balance between catharanthine and tabersonine/vindoline; no broader growth penalty reported.
Common_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: HL2 (parallel branch enzyme inferred in pathway context)
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: useful branch-control gene to direct flux toward catharanthine in metabolic engineering of anticancer alkaloid production
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: leaf epidermis-enriched EST dataset; GenBank accession MF770512

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: precursor for vindoline and downstream anticancer dimeric alkaloids
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: VIGS; in vitro pathway reconstitution with recombinant protein; metabolite profiling by LC-MS; assays with Tabernaemontana litoralis leaf proteins
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: second major hydrolase-family transcript in C. roseus leaves (about 35% of HL transcripts)
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: promotes tabersonine formation from O-acetylstemmadenine-derived reactive intermediate; silencing decreases vindoline/tabersonine-branch output and increases catharanthine
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. VIGS targeting HL2 reduced vindoline by about 65.5% and increased catharanthine to about 210%. 2. Addition of recombinant HL2 increased tabersonine formation by 100% in T. litoralis leaf protein assays. 3. HL2 is the branch enzyme for aspidosperma/tabersonine formation from the shared O-acetylated intermediate.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: VIGS in leaves; NADPH-dependent in vitro assays
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: High native HL2-like activity in C. roseus extracts complicated in vitro assignment, so T. litoralis extracts were used for stronger biochemical support.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Branch redirection can reduce catharanthine when flux is driven toward tabersonine; no growth penalty reported.
Common_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: HL1 (parallel branch enzyme inferred in pathway context)
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: valuable branch-selection gene to increase tabersonine/vindoline-line alkaloids in engineered systems
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: leaf epidermis-enriched EST dataset; GenBank accession MF770513

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: common upstream step leading to iboga/aspidosperma MIA formation
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: reduces 4,21-dehydrogeissoschizine-derived iminium intermediate to geissoschizine epimers
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: forms immediate upstream intermediate for catharanthine/tabersonine branch
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: catharanthine and tabersonine are precursors of medicinal MIAs including anticancer alkaloids
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: gateway enzyme controlling flux into iboga and aspidosperma MIA biosynthesis by producing geissoschizine
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: previous functional characterization cited in paper; recombinant expression in E. coli to produce 19E/19Z-geissoschizine; pathway reconstitution context in current study
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GS provides 19E-geissoschizine used as substrate for downstream pathway discovery. 2. It is the gateway step into iboga/aspidosperma MIA formation. 3. Current study incorporates GS into the eight-gene framework, but focuses experimental characterization downstream of 19E-geissoschizine.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Altering GS may broadly change flux among downstream MIA skeletons.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: essential upstream module for heterologous reconstruction of catharanthine/tabersonine and anticancer MIA production
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Gene was discovered previously by the authors and integrated here as the upstream entry point.

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: common pathway from geissoschizine to stemmadenine before iboga/aspidosperma branch split
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: unstable oxidized intermediate leading to short-lived m/z 369 species and, with Redox1/Redox2, stemmadenine
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: oxidizes 19E-geissoschizine to a highly unstable rearranged intermediate initiating conversion toward stemmadenine
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: forms immediate precursor/intermediate for stemmadenine and thus catharanthine/tabersonine
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: precursors to anticancer dimeric alkaloids and other bioactive MIAs
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: required for conversion of 19E-geissoschizine toward stemmadenine; silencing causes geissoschizine and side-product accumulation with major reduction in catharanthine/vindoline
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: VIGS; yeast microsome enzyme assay; LC-MS metabolite profiling; NMR identification of accumulated intermediates
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. VIGS of GO reduced catharanthine and vindoline and caused accumulation of 19E-geissoschizine, isositsirikine, pericyclivine, and perivine. 2. Recombinant GO converted 19E-geissoschizine to an oxidized short-lived intermediate and akuammicine. 3. GO functions as the first oxidative step in stemmadenine formation.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: key control step not formally established as rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Blocking GO diverts flux to alternative alkaloid skeletons such as sarpagan and corynanthean products.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: required oxidative module for reconstructing catharanthine/tabersonine pathways in heterologous hosts
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: leaf epidermis-enriched EST dataset; GenBank accession MF770508
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Originally annotated as CYP71AY2/CYP71D1v1 without known function before this study.

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: common pathway from geissoschizine to stemmadenine
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: unstable reduced intermediate leading to deshydroxymethylstemmadenine epimers in absence of Redox2
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: reduces a GO-generated iminium intermediate during conversion of geissoschizine toward stemmadenine
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: forms immediate precursor of stemmadenine, supplying common branch intermediate for catharanthine/tabersonine
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: required with GO and Redox2 for stemmadenine formation; silencing causes accumulation of akuammicine and a short-lived m/z 369 intermediate while reducing catharanthine/vindoline
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: VIGS; recombinant protein assays coupled with GO; LC-MS metabolite profiling; NMR characterization of accumulated metabolites
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. VIGS of Redox1 lowered catharanthine and vindoline and increased akuammicine plus a short-lived oxidized intermediate. 2. GO plus Redox1 converted 19E-geissoschizine to 16S/16R-DHS. 3. Redox1 performs the iminium reduction step between GO and Redox2 in stemmadenine biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Flux blockage increases breakdown/side products such as akuammicine.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: necessary redox module for productive stemmadenine formation in engineered pathways
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: leaf epidermis-enriched EST dataset; GenBank accession MF770509
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Mechanistically analogous to GS-type iminium reduction according to discussion.

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: common pathway from geissoschizine to stemmadenine
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: unstable Redox1-derived aldehyde intermediate; also 19E/19Z-geissoschizine for side reaction to isositsirikine
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: reduces an aldehyde intermediate to form stemmadenine; also stereospecifically reduces geissoschizine to 16R-isositsirikine
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: directly forms common precursor stemmadenine for catharanthine/tabersonine biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: required to complete stemmadenine formation; silencing causes accumulation of 16S- and 16R-DHS and lowers catharanthine/vindoline
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: VIGS; coupled recombinant assays with GO and Redox1; LC-MS; NMR analysis; yeast coexpression
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. VIGS of Redox2 caused accumulation of DHS epimers and reduced catharanthine/vindoline. 2. GO, Redox1, and Redox2 together produced stemmadenine. 3. Redox2 also catalyzed stereospecific reduction of geissoschizine to 16R-isositsirikine as a side route.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Can divert geissoschizine to isositsirikine side products depending on substrate context.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: required to obtain stemmadenine and avoid buildup of unstable intermediates in engineered systems
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: leaf epidermis-enriched EST dataset; GenBank accession MF770510
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Side activity explains isositsirikine accumulation in GO-silenced plants.

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: activation step before iboga/aspidosperma branch cyclization
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: transfers an acetyl group from acetyl-CoA to stemmadenine to form O-acetylstemmadenine
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: required for storage/modification/activation of precursor leading to catharanthine and tabersonine
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: activates stemmadenine for downstream conversion; O-acetylation is required for catharanthine/tabersonine biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: recombinant enzyme assay with purified protein; yeast coexpression; LC-MS; NMR identification of O-acetylstemmadenine; VIGS
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SAT converts stemmadenine to O-acetylstemmadenine in the presence of acetyl-CoA. 2. Acetyl-CoA addition enabled pathway completion from 19E-geissoschizine to catharanthine/tabersonine in crude protein assays. 3. SAT-mediated O-acetylation provides the activated substrate required before the NADPH-dependent cyclization branch step.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Silencing caused stemmadenine accumulation; broader penalties not reported.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: critical activation module for producing catharanthine/tabersonine in synthetic biology platforms
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: leaf epidermis-enriched EST dataset; GenBank accession MF770511
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: VIGS effect on final products was mild, likely due to incomplete silencing, but biochemical data strongly supported function.
